items.myth: Linear regression means the data must form a straight line in raw x–y space.
items.reality: The model is linear in parameters w, not necessarily in raw inputs. Polynomial, log, or interaction features can curve the fit while ŷ stays wᵀφ(x) + b.
items.example: Feature x² lets ŷ = w₁x + w₂x² + b trace a parabola — still "linear regression" in ML terminology.

items.myth: The normal equation and gradient descent always give different answers.
items.reality: Both minimize the same convex MSE objective. With exact arithmetic and full-rank X, they reach the same w*; differences come from numerical precision, learning rate, or early stopping.
items.example: NumPy lstsq and 500 SGD steps on the same toy X, y agree to ~1e-6 when η is tuned.

items.myth: High R² or low training MSE proves the model is correct for deployment.
items.reality: Training fit measures how well a line explains seen data, not causal truth or test performance. Leakage, nonlinearity, and out-of-range inputs break deployment silently.
items.example: R² = 0.99 on train but test RMSE doubles when seasonality shifts — model memorized noise.

items.myth: MSE is the only valid loss for linear regression.
items.reality: OLS uses MSE by default and links to Gaussian MLE, but LAD (MAE), Huber, and weighted least squares are legitimate when outliers or heteroscedasticity dominate — see Loss Functions.
items.example: One mis-recorded $50M sale: MAE-weighted or Huber loss reduces pull on w vs pure MSE.
